zotero.abstractNote: Human-AI collaboration often involves receiving advice from AI systems. This advice can be either global (e.g., a general strategy or rule) or local (e.g., a specific recommendation for a single decision). We investigate how the granularity of AI advice affects human decision-making in a sequential search task. Participants were tasked with finding the maximum value in a hidden landscape, receiving either global advice about the landscape's structure or local advice about the next best move. We find that while both types of advice improve performance compared to a no-advice baseline, they do so through different mechanisms. Global advice helps participants build a better mental model of the environment, leading to more exploratory behavior, whereas local advice acts as a direct heuristic, leading to more exploitative behavior. These results suggest that the optimal granularity of AI advice depends on the user's goal and the nature of the task.
zotero.conferenceName: 47th Annual Conference of the Cognitive Science Society
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Proceedings of the 47th Annual Conference of the Cognitive Science Society
